breakClass: left-join null member access / non-nullable value-column read on a possibly-null DefaultIfEmpty() entity in a server-side projection (EF Core 10 shaper reads SQL NULL into a non-nullable CLR value type â€” decimal/int/DateTime/enum â€” and throws; EF6 silently produced default(T))
why: POS Edit summary; same SEPayments left-join 500 risk as POSController.Create. Legacy identical.
finalFix:
In QuickNetCore/Controllers/POSController.cs, the `else`-branch projection (lines 1326-1327), null-guard the optional left-joined `d` to mirror EF6's default(decimal)=0 behavior:

    SEPaidAmount = d != null ? d.SEPaidAmount : 0,
    SEDueAmount = a.SEGrandTotal - (d != null ? d.SEPaidAmount : 0),

This is output-preserving: when a SEPayment exists, the value is unchanged; when `d` is null, it yields 0 / a.SEGrandTotal exactly as EF6 produced. (The walk-in `if`-branch at 1190-1222 needs no change since it does not read `d`.)
reasoning:
I could not refute this; it is a genuine EF Core 10 port-break, though severity is MEDIUM (edge-data), not high.

LOCATION: QuickNetCore/Controllers/POSController.cs:1326-1327, inside the POST action `public ActionResult Edit(POSViewModel vmodel, long id)` (signature at line 839), in the print/print_order reprint path, the `else` (non-walk-in customer) branch (lines 1303-1337).

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” CONFIRMED. The fragment is part of a single LINQ query expression: `(from a in db.SalesEntrys join b in db.Customers ... join d in db.SEPayments on a.SalesEntryId equals d.SalesEntry into pay from d in pay.DefaultIfEmpty() ... where a.SalesEntryId == salesEntryId select new { ... SEPaidAmount = d.SEPaidAmount, SEDueAmount = a.SEGrandTotal - d.SEPaidAmount, ... }).FirstOrDefault()`. There is NO .ToList()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary before the `select`; `.FirstOrDefault()` only terminates the IQueryable. The projection is therefore SQL-translated. `d` is an optional (DefaultIfEmpty/left-join) entity. It is NOT a group-aggregate over an IGrouping and NOT a standalone client-side local. Server-side confirmed.

(b) ENTITY/NULLABILITY â€” CONFIRMED. `d` aliases `db.SEPayments` (DbSet<SEPayment> at Models/ApplicationDbContext.cs:115, ordinary entity, not keyless). `SEPaidAmount` is a NON-NULLABLE `public decimal SEPaidAmount` on class SEPayment (Models/SalesEntry.cs:404). When the left join finds no match, `d` is null and the projected column `[d].[SEPaidAmount]` is SQL NULL. EF Core 10's relational shaper reads that NULL into a non-nullable CLR `decimal` and throws (InvalidOperationException / SqlNullValueException 'Data is Null'). `SEDueAmount = a.SEGrandTotal - d.SEPaidAmount` has the same defect.

(c) PORT REGRESSION â€” CONFIRMED. Legacy EF6 source _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/POSController.cs:647-648 is byte-identical (`SEPaidAmount = d.SEPaidAmount, SEDueAmount = a.SEGrandTotal - d.SEPaidAmount`). EF6's ObjectQuery materializer silently coalesced a NULL value-type read to default(decimal)=0; EF Core 10 throws. Classic shaper-behavior regression.

(d) SEVERITY = MEDIUM, not high. The POST Edit flow re-inserts a SEPayment row for the SalesEntry before this reprint query runs (the controller's create/edit logic does db.SEPayments.Add(SEpay) â€” see the analogous block at lines 402-424). So on the normal Editâ†’print happy path, `d` matches and is non-null: NO 500. It 500s ONLY on the edge case of an orphaned SalesEntry that has no SEPayment row. That edge IS reachable in this codebase: CustomerController.cs:4254 does db.SEPayments.Remove(...) for a single SalesEntry WITHOUT deleting the SalesEntry, and OrderRES/POSRES remove SEPayments. So the null-`d` row can exist and this projection will then throw.

NOTE: the parallel walk-in (`if`) branch at lines 1190-1222 left-joins SEPayments as `d` but never references `d` in its projection (uses SEPaidAmount = a.SEGrandTotal, SEDueAmount = 0), so it is SAFE â€” only the flagged `else` branch reads `d.SEPaidAmount`.

I set verdict=confirmed because the mechanism, server-side composition, port-regression, and edge reachability are all positively verified; wouldThrow500=true with the caveat that it fires on edge data (orphaned SEPayment), not the primary path.
